coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot decorated covalent organic framework for mild NIR-II photothermal and heterojunction amplified sonodynamic and chemodynamic therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Yamin
Fang, Lingling
Gu, Sizhen
Chen, Linjiao
Wu, Chenheng
Xue, Shigui
Tang, Yini
Dou, Danbo
Liu, Chenghai
coredata.dc:description: Albeit sonodynamic therapy (SDT) offers benefits such as high tissue-penetrating depth and favorable biosafety, the low-efficiency sonosensitizers and adverse tumor microenvironment (TME) still limit the antitumor effectiveness of SDT. In this study, we introduce the heterojunction-augmented and mild-hyperthermia-amplified sono-chemodynamic combination therapy strategy through endowing a theranostic nanoplatform with triple function including sonodynamic, chemodynamic, and NIR-II photothermal conversion properties. To demonstrate this strategy, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent NIR-II photothermal property are incorporated into the hollow covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) with sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities. The constructed CD/COF not only possesses improved the sono-chemodynamic performances of single-component material, but also exhibited enhanced photothermal effect, allowing for the achievement of mild NIR-II PTT. More importantly, the mild hyperthermia produced by CD/COF can enhance the performance of heterojunctions in sonodynamic and chemodynamic processes by relieving hypoxia and increasing catalytic activity. Notably, the single treatment of CD/COF heterojunctions successfully eradicates tumors through heterojunction and mild hyperthermia co-amplified SDT and chemodynamic therapy (CDT). Our strategy highlights the potential of simultaneously producing mild hyperthermia and fabricating heterojunction to amplify the efficacy of SDT and CDT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heterojunctions
Sonodynamic and chemodynamic therapy
Mild NIR-II photothermal therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) TEM and high-resolution TEM images of COF, CD, and CD/COF. (e–h) Survey XPS, high-resolution N 1s, C 1s, and O 1s spectra of CD/COF. (i–l) Absorption, FTIR, Zeta potential, and hydrodynamic diameter of CD, COF, and CD/COF. The concentration of CD, COF, and CD/CDF was 40 μg/mL. The solvent was DI water.

(a) Change of temperature of CD, COF, and CD/COF (400 μg/mL) exposed to 1064 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2). (b, c) Change of temperature of CD/COF exposed to 1064 nm laser at varied power density and varied concentrations. (d) Evaluation of photothermal stability of CD/COF. (e, f) Measurements of PCE of CD/COF. (g) Change of temperature of CD, COF, and CD/COF (400 μg/mL). (h) Change of temperature of CD/COF exposed to 808 nm laser. (i, j) Change of temperature of CD/COF exposed to 1064 or 808 nm laser in the presence of additional tissues with varied thickness. (k) Infrared thermal images of CD/COF exposed to 1064 nm.

(a–h) Evaluation of chemodynamic performance of CD/COF, COF, and CD in the presence of H2O2 at varied pH (6.0, 6.5, and 7.4). (i–l) Evaluation of chemodynamic performance of CD/COF, COF, and CD in the presence of 1064 nm laser irradiation. The concentration of CD/COF, COF, and CD was 40 μg/mL.

(a–d) Evaluation and comparison of 1O2 generation ability of CD/COF, COF, and CD. (e–h) VB-XPS spectrum and bandgap of COF and CD. (i) Schematic illustration of the bandgap structure of CD/COF. The concentration of CD/COF, COF, and CD was 40 μg/mL.

(a–d) Relative cell viability of LO2 and HCT-8 cells treated with CD/COF without or with US irradiation and 1064 nm laser irradiation. (e, f) Live/dead cell staining and ROS staining of HCT-8 cells after different treatments. The concentration of CD/COF and COF was 400 μg/mL.

(a) Shown in the diagram is the in vitro anticancer therapy of CD/COF with the help of mild NIR-II PTT-enhanced CDT/SDT. (b–d) Following the intravenous injection of CD/COF, NIR imaging was conducted of HCT-8 tumor-bearing mice. (e–h) Following different treatments, the tumor volume, survival outcomes, H&E staining results, and TUNEL staining findings were examined in mice. The dosage of COF and CD/COF was 4.0 mg/kg.
Schematic illustration of CD/COF construction for Z-scheme heterojunction-amplified and mild NIR-II photothermal-enhanced SDT and CDT.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot decorated covalent organic framework for mild NIR-II photothermal and heterojunction amplified sonodynamic and chemodynamic therapy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Albeit sonodynamic therapy (SDT) offers benefits such as high tissue-penetrating depth and favorable biosafety, the low-efficiency sonosensitizers and adverse tumor microenvironment (TME) still limit the antitumor effectiveness of SDT. In this study, we introduce the heterojunction-augmented and mild-hyperthermia-amplified sono-chemodynamic combination therapy strategy through endowing a theranostic nanoplatform with triple function including sonodynamic, chemodynamic, and NIR-II photothermal conversion properties. To demonstrate this strategy, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent NIR-II photothermal property are incorporated into the hollow covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) with sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities. The constructed CD/COF not only possesses improved the sono-chemodynamic performances of single-component material, but also exhibited enhanced photothermal effect, allowing for the achievement of mild NIR-II PTT. More importantly, the mild hyperthermia produced by CD/COF can enhance the performance of heterojunctions in sonodynamic and chemodynamic processes by relieving hypoxia and increasing catalytic activity. Notably, the single treatment of CD/COF heterojunctions successfully eradicates tumors through heterojunction and mild hyperthermia co-amplified SDT and chemodynamic therapy (CDT). Our strategy highlights the potential of simultaneously producing mild hyperthermia and fabricating heterojunction to amplify the efficacy of SDT and CDT.

Overall scheme to show the main synthesis procedures of CD/COF and their applications for Z-scheme heterojunction-amplified and mild NIR-II photothermal-enhanced SDT and CDT.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Health serves as the cornerstone of individuals' well-being, yet cancer has emerged as a significant threat to people's health [1]. The traditional clinical methods have notable side effects and specific risks to the human body [2,3]. Hence, it is crucial to investigate alternative safe and efficient approaches for tumor ablation. With the fast progress of theranostic nanomedicine, a wide range of therapeutic methods for cancer treatment have emerged, such as reactive oxygen species (ROS)-based treatment modalities [4–8]. Even though photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a well-established treatment capable of generating ROS, its effectiveness is hindered by the limited penetration depth [9–11]. For comparison, sonodynamic therapy (SDT) is a recent treatment method that involves using sonosensitizers activated by ultrasound to generate highly destructive ROS. This therapy has shown promising results in targeting deep-seated tumors due to the ability of ultrasound to penetrate to depths exceeding 10 cm [12–15]. Despite this, the effectiveness of SDT is still hindered by the low-efficiency sonosensitizers and complicated tumor microenvironment (TME) [16–19]. The inefficacy of organic sonosensitizers in SDT is ascribed to their limited water solubility and photostability [20,21]. Albeit inorganic sonosensitizers represented by TiO2 nanostructures offer improved chemical stability and a modifiable energy bandgap, the tendency for generated electrons and holes to recombine easily within the structure severely restrain their application prospects for SDT [22–26]. Consequently, it is imperative to seek a high-efficiency sonosensitizer with narrow bandgap and inhibited electron-hole recombination to overcome these barriers for enhanced SDT.

In addition to exploring high-efficiency sonosensitizer, the regulation and utilization of TME to break the redox balance is also crucial for enhancing the ROS generation efficiency during SDT [27–30]. The occurrence and growth of tumors in TME are known to be influenced by its intricate internal conditions. For example, the hypoxia within TME deteriorates the effectiveness of O2-dependent SDT [31–33]. In addition, glutathione (GSH) is highly expressed in TME where it serves as a defense mechanism against oxidative stress caused by ROS [34–37]. Approaches that deplete GSH levels and trigger ROS generation have been found to disturb the redox balance in the TME, ultimately boosting the antitumor effects through ROS accumulation [38–40]. Apart from hypoxia and overexpressed GSH in TME, another two features of TME can be exploited to amplify the ROS generation including acidity and overexpressed H2O2 of TME [41–44]. Chemodynamic therapy (CDT) is a treatment that converts the excessive H2O2 found in TME into •OH, effectively destroying cancer cells through Fenton-like reaction, which has several benefits including its superior catalytic ability for generating ROS and its reduced reliance on external stimulus [45–47]. Consequently, both SDT and CDT are favored over PDT for the management of tumors located deep within the body, as they excel in either penetrating deeply or overcoming the mentioned limitations by generating ROS in situ to combat cancer.

Based on exploring multifunctional nanomaterials with sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities, integrating CDT/SDT with other tumor therapeutic techniques like photothermal therapy (PTT) is expected to achieve a “1 + 1+1 > 3” therapeutic effect. PTT has revolutionized cancer therapy by offering advantages like localized treatment, noninvasiveness, and the ability to precisely control irradiation and temperature [48–50]. In contrast to photodynamic or sonodynamic therapy, PTT has the capability to directly eliminate tumor cells [51,52]. Nevertheless, PTT often encounters two bottlenecks. On the one hand, the typical operating range for common PTT agents is primarily focused on the shallow penetration depth of the NIR-I region, and few NIR-II photothermal conversion agents have been reported, which greatly limits the clinical translation of photothermal therapy [53,54]. On the other hand, the constant “always-on” feature and the excessive operating temperature (>55 °C) of PTT may lead to irreparable harm to healthy tissues around the tumor even within the NIR-II range [55–58]. If the photothermal effect is decreased, however, the effectiveness of PTT is significantly diminished [59–61]. Hence, we hypothesized that mild hyperthermia (approximately 43 °C) could serve as a supplementary therapeutic approach, as opposed to directly destroying cancer cells. As expected, the mild hyperthermia can not only enhance the catalytic activity of CDT agents, but also alleviate tumor hypoxia to provide more O2 for SDT. Hence, endowing multifunctional sonosensitizers and CDT agents with exceptional NIR-II photothermal characteristics may facilitate SDT and CDT through mild hyperthermia, thus addressing the limitations of a single mode.

Currently, it is still a challenge to endow a theranostic nanoplatform with triple function including sonodynamic, chemodynamic, and NIR-II photothermal conversion properties. To substantiate the hypothesis proposed above, in this work, we introduce the first design of a simplified tumor therapy nanoplatform that incorporates carbon dots (CDs) with excellent NIR-II photothermal property and hollow covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) with sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities (Scheme 1 ). The COFs with hollow microspherical morphology were synthesized by the template-etching strategy using p-phthalaldehyde and 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene as the precursors. Because of the quick carrier transfer rate and the prevention of electron-hole pair recombination, the sonodynamic and chemodynamic performances of COF were amplified by the construction of Z-scheme heterojunctions after the decoration of CDs in the hollow COF. In addition, the fabricated CD/COF heterojunctions also exhibited enhanced NIR-II photothermal property, which can easily achieve mild NIR-II PTT at a safe power density of 0.6 W/cm2 after irradiating for 3 min. The mild hyperthermia produced by CD/COF can further improve the sonodynamic and chemodynamic performances of heterojunctions owing to the relieving hypoxia and enhanced catalytic activity. By leveraging these beneficial characteristics, the tumor was successfully eliminated through a cascade amplification of ROS production triggered by CD/COF. This was achieved thanks to the combination of heterojunction-amplified and mild NIR-II hyperthermia-enhanced SDT and CDT.

Through a straightforward microwave reaction, CDs were obtained from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and spermidine. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image revealed the torispherical morphology of CDs (Fig. 1 a), which also indicated that the average size of CDs was 3.3 ± 0.1 nm. The size determined by TEM image was similar to that measured by DLS (Fig. 1l). We also utilized the high-resolution TEM image to observe the crystallinity of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. S1), which illustrated that the lattice spacing of CDs was approximately 0.21 nm. Fig. S2 showed that a broad diffraction peak at about 22° corresponded to the (002) plane of CDs. The survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum (Fig. S3) revealed the N-doped characteristics of CDs, with the presence of N, C, and O elements being identified. The presence of N element in CDs could be ascribed to the containing-N precursor of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and spermidine. Graphitic N can be identified in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum of CDs, indicating that CDs may exhibit exceptional photothermal conversion capabilities as demonstrated in our previous studies [62]. Additionally, the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) indicated the existence of C–N bonds, further supporting their N-doped features of CDs (Fig. 1j). Owing to the presence of pyridine N in the edge of CDs and the protonation of pyridine N, CDs exhibited a strong positively charged feature (Fig. 1k).

In the synthesis procedure of the hollow COF, the hollow COF was produced using a template of aminated silica microspheres (NH2–SiO2) and NaOH as the etchant. The imine bond was formed between the NH2–SiO2 microsphere with amino group and p-phthalaldehyde with aldehyde groups. This allowed for the uniform growth of the layered COF on the surface of the NH2–SiO2 microsphere. Afterwards, we used a NaOH solution to remove the NH2–SiO2 template, but the hollow COF was able to remain intact due to the exceptional stability of COF in alkaline solutions. The successful preparation of the hollow COF was verified by the TEM image (Fig. 1b), which revealed a hollow microspherical structure of COF. The size of the hollow COF determined by TEM image was about 220 nm, which was similar to the DLS result (234.8 nm) (Fig. 1l). The presence of amino group, imine bond, and aldehyde group in COF can be detected in the FTIR and XPS spectrum (Fig. 1j and S4). Owing to the presence of imine bond, COF exhibited a strong negatively charged feature (Fig. 1k).

Because of the electrostatic interaction, CD/COF heterojunctions were ultimately synthesized by mixing and stirring CDs and COF. Through measuring the weight change of COF before and after CD loading, the loading efficiency of CDs in COF was measured to be approximately 37 %. TEM image illustrated that a large number of CDs was loaded inside the COF (Fig. 1c). The high-resolution TEM image depicted in Fig. 1d further confirmed the loading of CDs, which revealed that the lattice fringe of CDs can be detected. The size of heterojunctions was detected to be approximately 269.3 nm (Fig. 1l), which was slightly higher than that of pristine COF, further demonstrating the successful construction of CD/COF heterojunctions. After assembly, the zeta potential of COF was reversed owing to the loading of CDs (Fig. 1k). Next, the successful deposition of CDs inside COF was demonstrated by the various characterization techniques, including FTIR, XRD, and XPS analysis. The stretching vibrations of C–H attributed to COF can be also detected in the CD/COF heterojunctions (Fig. 1j). In addition, a broad diffraction peak of CDs located at 22° can be also observed in the CD/COF heterojunctions (Fig. S2). Moreover, the presence of amino group, CN, and C–N can be detected in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum of heterojunctions (Fig. 1f), which was consistent with that of the pristine COF. Apart from the high-resolution N 1s, the high-resolution O 1s and C 1s presented in Fig. 1g, h can also indicate the successful fabrication of heterojunctions owing to the existence of CO, C–OH, CC, C–C, C–N, and C–H in the CD/COF heterojunctions. Besides, the characteristic absorption peaks of COF and CDs were also detected in the UV–vis–NIR absorption spectrum of heterojunctions (Fig. 1i). We then investigated the dispersibility and stability of CD/COF in aqueous phases and physiological environments. As depicted in Fig. S5, the clear and transparent solution of CD/COF can be detected in the medium of DI water, DMEM, and FBS after storing for 7 days, demonstrating the excellent dispersibility and stability of CD/COF. We also investigated the stability of CD/COF at pH 6.0. As presented in Fig. S6, no absorption change and solution color change can be detected in the CD/COF solution at pH 6.0 after storing for 7 days, indicating the good stability of CD/COF in the acidic condition.

After confirming the successful synthesis of CD/COF, we then proceeded to evaluate the photothermal properties by determining the elevated temperature and conversion efficiency (η). Initially, the higher absorbance at 1064 nm of CD/COF compared with the pristine CDs and COF can be detected in Fig. 1i, which could be ascribed to the formation of heterojunctions. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, when subjected to the same conditions, the heterojunction group experienced a significantly higher temperature rise than the CD or COF groups. Moreover, the photothermal properties of CD/COF were assessed based on their concentration and power density, with both factors demonstrating a direct relationship with the rise in temperature (Fig. 2b and c). It is worth noting that the mild hyperthermia was successfully triggered at 0.6 W/cm2, resulting in a temperature increase of the CD/COF solution to around 43 °C after irradiating for 3 min. Furthermore, the infrared thermographic images provided evidence of the excellent photothermal property of heterojunctions (Fig. 2k). Apart from utilizing the 1064 nm laser, the superior photothermal capabilities of heterojunctions compared to individual CD and COF were also confirmed to be using an 808 nm laser (Fig. 2g, h, and S8-S10).

To provide a more detailed analysis of the heightened photothermal capabilities of CD/COF, we measured and calculated the conversion efficiency of both CD and CD/COF. The conversion efficiency of the CD and CD/COF were about 88.99 % and 94.79 %, respectively (Fig. 2e and f). This forcefully substantiated the significantly enhanced photothermal performance of COF following the assembly of the heterojunctions. The enhanced conversion efficiency of CD/COF can be also detected when using 808 nm laser, which was determined to approximately 84.21 % (Fig. S11), higher than that of single-component CDs (73.05 %). Notably, we conducted a comparison of the conversion efficiency of the CD/COF heterojunctions with other PTT agents that have been previously reported. Our findings revealed that the conversion efficiency of CD/COF at 1064 nm or 808 nm surpassed that of noble metal and metal oxygen/sulfide. The photothermal effect of CD/COF was synergistically amplified, demonstrating that the formation of heterojunctions could accelerate the carrier transfer rate, ultimately enhancing the photothermal effects of the individual components significantly. In addition, the photothermal stability of CD/COF was examined over ten heating/cooling cycles using 1064 or 808 nm laser, showing no significant temperature degradation (Fig. 2d and S8b).

Subsequently, the CD/COF was examined for its potential to convert light into heat within deep tissues, mimicking a clinical situation. Even with an extra 10 mm of tissue coverage, CD/COF can still achieve a more than 20 °C of temperature increase when treated with a 1064 nm laser (Fig. 2i), while only 5 °C of temperature increase can be realized when exposed to a 808 nm laser (Fig. 2j), which demonstrated the excellent deep-tissue photothermal performance of CD/COF-mediated NIR-II PTT. This phenomenon also indicated that the mild NIR-II hyperthermia can be easily obtained by CD/COF-mediated NIR-II PTT even in the presence of additional tissues.

Having confirmed the enhanced photothermal property of heterojunctions, we then investigated the heterojunction and mild NIR-II hyperthermia amplified chemodynamic activity of CD/COF. As the concentration of H2O2 increased, the absorbance of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) also increased in the presence of the CD/COF, indicating effective chemodynamic activity at a slightly acidic pH of 6.0, as shown in Fig. 3 a. Additionally, the CD/COF group exhibited a significantly higher TMB absorption compared to both the CD and COF groups (Fig. 3b and c), signifying the heterojunction-amplified chemodynamic activity. To further measure the improved chemodynamic performance of heterojunctions, the rate constant of •OH was calculated to compare the chemodynamic activity of CD/COF, CD, and COF. Fig. 3d illustrated that the CD/COF exhibit a rate constant of 0.257 min−1, surpassing the values of single-component CD (0.074 min−1) and COF (0.222 min−1). The chemodynamic performance of the CD/COF at varied pH was also evaluated to determine how pH influences catalytic activity. As depicted in Fig. 3e–g, CD/COF, CD, and COF all exhibited no •OH generation at pH 6.5. In addition to pH 6.5, a similar phenomenon can be detected at pH 7.4 (Fig. 3h and S12), which can guarantee the safety of CD/COF owing to the insignificant ROS generation in normal physiological conditions. It was proven through these findings that the chemodynamic activity of COF could be greatly enhanced by forming heterojunctions.

Then, we explored the mild photothermal influence on the chemodynamic property of CD/COF heterojunctions following treatment with a 1064 nm laser. Fig. 3i demonstrated that the absorbance of TMB in CD/COF +1064 group outperformed that in CD/COF group, indicating that the Fenton-like catalytic activity of heterojunctions could be magnified by the mild NIR-II hyperthermia. In addition, a similar enhancement effect can be observed in the single-component CD and COF, which also revealed that the mild NIR-II hyperthermia can improve the chemodynamic performance of CD and COF (Fig. 3j and k). The rate constant for •OH production was specifically observed to be greater in the heterojunction group than in the other groups when NIR-II laser was present (as shown in Fig. 3l). This suggested that by forming a heterojunction and implementing mild hyperthermia, there is the possibility of achieving a cascade amplification of ROS generation efficiency.

Subsequently, we analyzed the sonodynamic activity of CD/COF heterojunctions when subjected to US irradiation, with 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) serving as a colorimetric indicator. The drop in the characteristic absorption peak of DPBF in CD/COF subjected to US treatment (50 kHz, 1.0 W/cm2) was found to be more significant than that of COF and CDs, as depicted in Fig. 4 a–c, suggesting that heterojunctions produced more 1O2. Moreover, the rate constant for 1O2 generation in the CD/COF group under US irradiation was 0.033 min−1 (Fig. 4d), which was 1.4 times greater than that in COF alone group (0.024 min−1). In addition to 1O2, we also investigated that the O2 −• production capability of CD/COF, COF, and CDs using Dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR123) as the probe. As depicted in Fig. S13, the significantly increased fluorescence intensity of DHR123 can be detected in the CD/COF group compared with the COF group and the CD group, suggesting the enhanced O2 −• generation ability of heterojunctions. These results demonstrated that CD/COF also exhibit enhanced sonodynamic activity in addition to their improved chemodynamic activity and NIR-II photothermal performance.

To shed light on the enhanced sonodynamic mechanism, we conducted a study on the energy band structure of CD/COF, focusing on their bandgap and valence band potential. Fig. 4g, h illustrated the calculation of bandgaps for CD and COF from their absorption spectra, demonstrating a narrow bandgap for CD (1.9 eV) and COF (2.2 eV). The potential of valence band (VB) of CD and COF was measured by the VB-XPS spectrum, which was determined to be 1.58 and 2.48 eV, respectively (Fig. 4e and f). Following this, by utilizing the valence band potential and bandgap values, the conduction band (CB) potentials for CD and COF can be determined as −0.32 and 0.28 eV, respectively. Considering that CD and COF possessed positively and negatively charged features, the presence of electric field within CD/COF heterojunctions can be demonstrated. In addition, the direction of the electric field was from CD to COF, thereby illustrating that the direction of electron transfer was from COF to CD (Fig. 4i). Consequently, CD/COF exhibited the Z-scheme heterojunction characteristics, which could greatly inhibit the potential recombination of electron and hole after activating by US irradiation and then amplifying the ROS generation efficiency.

Given the heterojunction and mild NIR-II hyperthermia improved sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities of CD/COF, we next attempted to investigate the in vitro antitumor efficacy of heterojunctions. Initially, the cellular uptake behavior of CD/COF was assessed after labeling by Indocyanine Green (ICG) dye. As shown in Fig. S14, ICG-labeled CD/COF displayed efficient cellular uptake ability, which exhibited the strong red fluorescence signal in the cytoplasm of HCT-8 cells. The MTT assay then confirmed the good biocompatibility of the three samples, which suggested that no significant cytotoxicity of CD/COF, CD, and COF against human normal liver LO2 cells can be detected even at the concentration of 400 μg/mL (Fig. 5 a and S15). For comparison, a slight cytotoxicity of CD/COF and COF against HCT-8 cells was detected at the same incubation conditions (Fig. 5b and S16), which could be ascribed to the CDT effect in the cancer cells. When US irradiation employed, approximately 70 % HCT-8 cells were dead after treating with CD/COF (Fig. 5c), which could be attributed to the synergistic effect of SDT and CDT. In contrast, the cytotoxicity of CD/COF + US was higher than that of COF + US and CD + US (Fig. S17), demonstrating the heterojunction-amplified sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities. Moreover, the CD/COF + US + 1064 group showed the highest therapeutic effect, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d. This group effectively eliminated almost all HCT-8 cells using a combination of mild NIR-II PTT, SDT, and CDT. Like the combination SDT and CDT, the therapeutic benefit of mild NIR-II PTT combined with SDT and CDT via heterojunctions was greater compared to using single-component CD and COF alone (Fig. S18).

Apart from the MTT assay, live/dead cell staining was also performed. As depicted in Fig. 5e, all HCT-8 cells were stained with green fluorescence in the control and US alone groups, suggesting that no cytotoxicity can be detected when the US irradiation applied alone. Furthermore, a larger number of HCT-8 cells showed red fluorescence in the CD/COF + US group rather than the COF + US group, implying that the development of heterojunctions could improve the efficacy of both SDT and CDT in pristine COF. Notably, no green fluorescence signal can be observed in the CD/COF + US + 1064 group, suggesting that all HCT-8 cells were dead after the treatment of heterojunction and mild hyperthermia amplified SDT/CDT via CD/COF, which was consistent with the MTT results.

After conducting various treatments, we proceeded to perform ROS staining on HCT-8 cells to assess the levels of intracellular ROS. Fig. 5f revealed that HCT-8 cells treated with control and US alone did not exhibit green fluorescence signal, demonstrating that US alone treatment would not produce ROS for the SDT. The higher ROS level can be detected in CD/COF + US compared with the COF + US, which could be ascribed to the heterojunction-improved sonodynamic and chemodynamic activities. The CD/COF + US + 1064 group exhibited the most intense green fluorescence signal among all the groups, providing strong evidence that combining mild NIR-II PTT with the creation of a heterojunction structure in CD and COF can lead to a significant increase in ROS production through cascade amplification.

Encouraged by the positive outcomes of CD/COF-mediated mild NIR-II PTT and heterojunction-amplified SDT/CDT in cell studies, we then moved on to animal trials to assess their efficacy in combating tumors in living organisms. To monitor the tumor accumulation ability of CD/COF, we utilized ICG dye to label heterojunctions and the in vivo NIR imaging was carried out. After administration of ICG@CD/COF, the gradually increased fluorescence signal was observed in the tumor tissues (Fig. 6 b). Specifically, the strongest signal can be detected at 24 h post-injection (Fig. 6c), suggesting that the optimum US irradiation can be performed at this time point. To demonstrate this in vivo NIR imaging result, we conducted ex vivo NIR imaging of the major organs and tumors after intravenous injection of ICG@CD/COF. As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the higher accumulation of CD/COF was detected in the tumor tissues compared with the other organs, demonstrating the good passive targeting ability of heterojunctions. The fluorescence signal of CD/COF in tumor tissues is most pronounced at 24 h post-injection, mirroring the in vivo NIR imaging findings (Fig. S19). In addition to tumor tissues, the significant fluorescence signal of ICG@CD/COF can be detected in the liver and kidney, suggesting that CD/COF was eliminated from the mice through the liver and kidney. Additionally, we compared the pharmacokinetic property of pristine CD and CD/COF to demonstrate the enhanced passive targeting ability of heterojunctions. By deposition of CDs inside hollow COF nanospheres, the elimination half-life of the pristine CDs was increased from 0.23 ± 0.15 h to 2.44 ± 0.29 h (Fig. S20), demonstrating that the incorporation of CDs into hollow COF nanospheres could improve the tumor accumulation level of CDs owing to the prolonged circulation time.

Subsequently, we developed the subcutaneous tumor model to assess the therapeutic outcome of heterojunctions and mild NIR-II photothermal enhanced SDT and CDT. After being administered an intravenous injection of CD/COF for 24 h, the mice underwent irradiation using a mild NIR-II laser and US (Fig. 6a). Upon examination of the infrared thermal image in Fig. S21, it was observed that the temperature of tumor tissues can be elevated to approximately 43 °C after a 3-min irradiation with a 1064 nm laser (0.6 W/cm2), showcasing the effectiveness of mild NIR-II PTT. The rapid increase of tumor volume can be detected in the control and US alone groups (Fig. 6e), verifying the insignificant antitumor effectiveness of the US irradiation. Conversely, the COF + US group showed a slight inhibitory effect on tumor growth, which could be explained by the synergistic effect of SDT and CDT via the pristine COF. The higher inhibitory effect on the tumor volume can be detected in the CD/COF + US group. Significantly, the tumors in CD/COF + US + 1064 group were eliminated after treating them for 14 days, demonstrating that the sonodynamic and chemodynamic activity can be further amplified by the mild hyperthermia. In addition, as seen in Fig. 6f, every mouse in the CD/COF + US + 1064 group lived for more than 50 days. The enhanced antitumor effectiveness of CD/COF + US + 1064 was also demonstrated by the histological analysis. Fig. 6h exhibited that the apoptosis and necrosis tumor cells can be detected in CD/COF + US + 1064.

Ultimately, the long-term toxicity was examined in vivo. While receiving simultaneous US and 1064 nm laser irradiation after CD/COF administration, the mice's body weight remained stable throughout the treatment period, as demonstrated in Fig. 6g, suggesting the insignificant side effects of these treatments. We also performed the hemolysis assay of the RBCs to carefully investigate the biocompatibility of CD/COF. As depicted in Fig. S22, no obvious hemolysis of the RBCs can be detected in CD/COF even at the concentration of 400 μg/mL, indicating that CD/COF would not influence the RBCs. Additionally, on day 14, there were no significant signs of inflammation or pathology observed in the H&E staining (Fig. S23), thus affirming the excellent biosafety of CD/COF. Following this, hematological tests were performed, which consisted of blood routine and blood biochemistry examinations, to determine the long-term toxicity of CD/COF in vivo. Fig. S24 revealed that the primary indicators of liver and kidney function in mice following various treatments fell within normal parameters, suggesting no significant inflammation throughout the treatment durations. From these findings, it is apparent that the combination of systemic CD/COF administration with concurrent US and 1064 nm irradiation offers remarkable biosafety in vivo.

In summary, incorporating CDs inside hollow COF nanospheres successfully led to the construction of CD/COF heterojunctions, which showed high-efficiency triple function including sonodynamic, chemodynamic, and NIR-II photothermal conversion properties. The accelerated transfer rate of carriers and the suppression of electron-hole pair recombination led to an amplification of the chemodynamic and sonodynamic performances of COF, achieved by forming heterojunctions with the decoration of CDs within the hollow COF. Additionally, the development of Z-scheme heterojunctions may significantly improve the photothermal conversion efficiency of CD/COF, demonstrating a higher photothermal conversion efficiency of 94.79 % at 1064 nm compared with the pristine CDs (88.99 %). Significantly, the improved sonodynamic and chemodynamic capabilities of CD/COF can be further enhanced by the mild NIR-II photothermal effect due to the relieved hypoxia TME and amplified catalytic activity. Ultimately, the presence of a mild NIR-II laser and US irradiation enabled CD/COF to completely suppress tumor growth through the cascade amplification of ROS generation. Our work will provide a novel prospect of the construction of heterojunctions and introduction of mild NIR-II PTT for the achievement of amplified SDT and CDT.

Preparation of CDs. A quick and easy microwave reaction was used to synthesize CDs exhibiting excellent NIR-II photothermal effect from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and spermidine. In brief, 0.025 g of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and 0.1 g of spermidine were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water. The mixture was transferred to a microwave reaction tube and heated at 200 °C for 15 min. After being cooled to room temperature, the CDs were dialyzed for 48 h to obtain the pure CDs.

Preparation of hollow COF nanospheres. For the preparation of NH2–SiO2, 1 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate was dissolved in 60 mL of ethanol. The mixture was then mixed with 30 mL of DI water, 20 mL of isopropyl alcohol, and 15 mL of ammonium hydroxide solution. After stirring, it was evident that there was a noticeable white turbidity in the solution, following which 7 mL of TEOS was added dropwise. Following a 4-h stirring period, 10 % of TEOS in (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane was added to the mixture, which was then left to react for another 2 h.

For the preparation of NH2–SiO2/COF, the mixture consisted of 100 mg of NH2–SiO2, 20 mg of p-phthalaldehyde, and 40 mg of 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene, which were dissolved in a solution containing 10 mL 1,4-dioxane and mesitylene (v/v = 4:1).

For the preparation of hollow COF, NH2–SiO2/COF was mixed with sodium hydroxide solution (10 mL, 1 M). The hollow COF nanospheres were obtained after centrifugation and washed with DI water for five times.

Preparation of CD/COF heterojunctions. 10 mL of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) was mixed with 5 mL of COF (0.5 mg/mL). The CD/COF heterojunctions were finally obtained after centrifugation and washing with DI water for five times. The unloaded CDs were removed after centrifugation.

Photothermal property measurements. By employing an 808 nm or 1064 nm laser, we irradiated the CDs, COF, and CD/COF solution for 300 s and tracked their temperature changes with an infrared thermal camera. The PCE was calculated based on the previous reports [62]. In brief, after the irradiation of 1064 or 808 nm laser for 5 min, the centrifuge tube containing CD/COF and CDs was cooled to room temperature. We utilized an infrared thermal imaging camera to measure the temperature of solution during the heating and cooling period. Then, we utilized the following four equations.
(1)
η =
h A
(
T max
−
T
s u r r
)
−
Q
D i s
I
(
1 −
10
− A λ
)
(2)
θ =
T −
T
s u r r
T max
−
T
s u r r
(3)
τ s
=
m D
c D
h A
(4)
t = − τ
ln
( θ )

To assess the photothermal properties of deep-tissue, chicken breast tissues of different thicknesses (0–10 mm) were coated with a CD/COF solution. Then, the irradiation was performed for the same time (5 min) and the temperature of the solution was determined.

Sonodynamic performance evaluation. At a frequency of 50 kHz and intensity of 1.0 W/cm2, DPBF (20 μg) was combined with CD, COF, and a CD/COF solution at a concentration of 0.4 mg/mL before being subjected to ultrasound irradiation. Throughout the ultrasound irradiation process, the absorption of the combined solution was tracked to evaluate the effectiveness of ROS production.

Chemodynamic performance evaluation. The evaluation of the chemodynamic activity of CDs, COF, and CD/COF required the measurement of absorption changes in TMB. As a representation, 20 μL of TMB was blended with CDs, COF, and CD/COF at the same concentration of 400 μg/mL, while the concentration of H2O2 was altered from 0 to 2.0 mM.

Cellular experiments. LO2 or HCT-8 cells were purchased from Haixing Biosciences (Suzhou, China). To conduct the MTT assay, either LO2 or HCT-8 cells were first plated in a 96-well plate. After adding CD, COF, and CD/COF samples with varying concentrations into the well, they were left to incubate for either 24 or 48 h. MTT agent was then added to each well for the incubation of 4 h. Finally, the DMSO was added to each well and the absorbance at 490 nm was measured to calculate the cell viability of LO2 and HCT-8 cells. The irradiation conditions of US and 1064 nm laser were 50 kHz (1.0 W/cm2) and 0.6 W/cm2, respectively.

In vivo antitumor effectiveness evaluation. The nude mice, aged five weeks, were purchased from SLAC in Shanghai, China. The animal experiments were carried out in accordance with the permission granted by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shuguang Hospital. Once the tumor volume reached around 100 mm3, the HCT-8 tumor-bearing mice were divided into five groups: control, US, COF + US, CD/COF + US, and CD/COF + US + 1064. The dosage of COF and CD/COF was 4.0 mg/kg. Following treatment with US and 1064 laser for 24 h, tumor tissues were subjected to H&E staining and TUNEL analysis. Upon completion of the 14-day treatment regimen, major organs and tumors were gathered for histopathological analysis and hematological tests. The tumor volume and body weight were measured every day during the treatment period.

Statistical analysis. All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. All experiments were performed with at least three independent replicates. All data were reported as the mean ± standard deviation (SD).

Yamin Pan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lingling Fang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Sizhen Gu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Linjiao Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chenheng Wu: Methodology, Investigation. Shigui Xue: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yini Tang: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology. Danbo Dou: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Chenghai Liu: Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
